meta.title: S214 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes E-Class S214 engine — OM654, M254, M256, OM656. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and hybrid tech explained. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E-Class S214 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S214 engine typically costs £3,000–£15,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the volume OM654 diesel and M254 petrol sit at £3,000–£6,000, while the M256-based AMG E 53 Hybrid unit ranges from £10,000–£15,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes S214 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.820 diesel (E 220d) and the M256.830 straight-six petrol (E 450) are the most dependable. The OM654 is a proven, robust diesel introduced in 2016, while the M256 has been in production since 2017 with a strong reputation for refinement and reliability. The M254 four-cylinder petrol is newer but generally reliable, though its long-term record is still being established.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E-Class S214?

acceptedAnswer.text: 48V mild-hybrid system issues including ISG and battery failures, PHEV battery degradation on the 25.4 kWh unit, software and electrical glitches affecting MBUX Superscreen and driver assistance systems, and — unrelated to the engine — air suspension failures on Airmatic-equipped estates.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E-Class S214 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S214 is too new for definitive long-term reliability data, but the underlying engine families (OM654, M256, M254) are well-established. The OM654 and M256 have strong reputations, while the M254 is still being proven. The 48V mild-hybrid and PHEV systems introduce additional complexity — early signs are positive, but these are new technologies for the E-Class estate.

mainEntity.name: Which S214 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the M254-based PHEVs (E 300e, E 350e, E 400e) and the E 300de PHEV diesel carry the highest long-term risk due to battery degradation and complex hybrid systems. If you're buying used, have the battery health checked by a Mercedes dealer. The AMG E 53 Hybrid is also highly complex and expensive to maintain.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S214 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: 48V system warning lights, loss of electric assistance, or ISG failure (all models), reduced electric range or battery warnings (PHEV variants), or check engine lights with drivability issues are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class S214 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S214 is a current-generation car with strong retained values — a £3,500–£5,500 OM654 recon against a £25,000–£30,000 E 220d is 12–18% of the car's value, safely below our 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the M254 at £3,000–£6,000 against a £28,000 E 300 is 11–21%. The only exception is the AMG E 53 Hybrid — but if you can afford a £70,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance. Engine replacement is generally viable across the range, but PHEV battery replacement is a separate, significant cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes E-Class S214 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel and M256 straight-six are expected to exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing, based on their track records in previous models. The M254 is newer, but early signs suggest it should reach 150,000+ miles. The PHEV battery will degrade over time — expect 80–100,000 miles before noticeable range reduction, though the 8-year/100,000-mile warranty provides some protection.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S214, W214 and X214?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three are the sixth-generation E-Class. The S214 is the 5-door estate (2023–present, this page), the W214 is the 4-door saloon (2023–present), and the X214 is the All-Terrain (2023–present) with raised ride height and off-road styling. They share the same platform, engines, and most components — but the estate has the 25.4 kWh battery in the boot floor (on PHEV variants) and additional load-carrying capacity. Costs vary slightly by body style — see the W214 and X214 generation pages for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E-Class S214 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., E 220d, E 300, E 450) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class S214 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S214 estate — from the OM654 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesels to the M254 2.0-litre petrols, the M256 3.0-litre straight-sixes, and the M256-based AMG E53 Hybrid. All S214 engines are four- or six-cylinder units with 48V mild-hybrid or plug-in hybrid assistance — Mercedes has electrified the entire E-Class range. Backed by real UK S214 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class S214 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S214 estate specifically — all are mild hybrid or plug-in hybrid units, marking the first time the E-Class estate has been offered exclusively with electrified powertrains.

engines.power: 204 hp engine + 129 hp motor = 313 hp combined
252 hp engine + 129 hp motor = 381 hp combined
443 hp engine + 161 hp motor = 604 hp combined
197 hp engine + 129 hp motor = 313 hp combined

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: M256 straight-six: 80% confidence — proven engine since 2017 with a strong reputation for refinement and reliability. OM654 four-cylinder diesel: 80% confidence — introduced in 2016 as Mercedes' first all-aluminium diesel, significantly lighter than its predecessor. M254 four-cylinder petrol: 75% confidence — newer engine replacing the M264, with some reported issues being understood. OM656 straight-six diesel: 80% confidence — introduced in 2017, one of the most powerful diesel engines Mercedes has produced. PHEV variants: 70% confidence — the 25.4 kWh battery and complex hybrid systems are still being proven in the real world.

overview.intro: The S214 is the estate body style of the sixth-generation E-Class, launched in May 2023 as the successor to the S213 estate. Based on the MRA2 platform, it features a longer wheelbase and increased interior space. For the first time, every S214 E-Class is equipped with an electrified powertrain — 48V mild hybrid or plug-in hybrid — with no purely combustion-engined options available. The engine range spans the OM654 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel, the M254 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol, the M256 3.0-litre straight-six petrol, and the OM656 3.0-litre straight-six diesel. Plug-in hybrid options (E 300e, E 350e, E 400e, E 300de, AMG E 53 Hybrid) use a 25.4 kWh battery offering up to 71 miles of all-electric range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA2 • Predecessor: S213 • Successor: — (current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W214 Saloon, X214 All-Terrain.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S214 E-Class estate is the current-generation model; enquiry volumes are growing as early 2023–2024 cars exit manufacturer warranty and enter the used market.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre OM656 is one of the most powerful diesel engines Mercedes has ever produced — 367 hp and 750 Nm of torque, with 48V mild-hybrid assistance. It's a refined, smooth straight-six that delivers effortless performance while maintaining genuine economy. Introduced in 2017, the OM656 has proven itself as a robust, durable engine.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless diesel torque and six-cylinder refinement without the complexity of a V8.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre M256 straight-six is a masterpiece of modern engineering — 381 hp, 500 Nm of torque, and the smoothest six-cylinder petrol Mercedes has made since the M104. Introduced in 2017, it replaced the M276 V6 and has proven itself as a refined, reliable powerplant. The 48V mild-hybrid system and integrated starter-generator deliver seamless start-stop and electric boost.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the pinnacle of petrol refinement in an E-Class estate.

items.quote: The M254 four-cylinder petrol is a newer engine, and the complex 25.4 kWh plug-in hybrid system adds significant complexity. The combination of combustion engine, electric motor, and hybrid battery creates more potential failure points than a conventional powertrain. While early signs are positive, long-term reliability data is limited.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise low company-car tax and electric commuting over long-term simplicity.

items.quote: The AMG E 53 Hybrid's powertrain is the most complex ever fitted to an E-Class estate — a 3.0-litre M256M straight-six with an electric auxiliary compressor, a 161 hp electric motor, and a 25.4 kWh battery, producing 604 hp combined. When something goes wrong, it goes wrong expensively. The electric turbocharger, the hybrid system, and the sheer labour intensity of working on this engine mean replacement costs will be eye-watering.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want the most powerful E-Class estate ever.

items.quote: The E 220d is the volume diesel for good reason — 197 hp, 440 Nm of torque, genuine 50+ mpg economy, and the same robust OM654 as the more powerful variants. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to run, and cheaper to replace than the six-cylinder diesels. The sweet spot in the S214 range for most UK buyers.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best combination of economy, performance, and reliability in the S214 range.

items.quote: The E 300de is the most overlooked engine in the S214 range — a 2.0-litre OM654 diesel combined with a 129 hp electric motor and 25.4 kWh battery, delivering 313 hp combined and up to 71 miles of electric range. It's the ultimate company car choice — low BIK tax with diesel economy for long journeys.
items.whoItsFor: company car drivers who need the lowest possible tax bills with genuine long-distance capability.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol (Value) — proven M254, mild-hybrid refinement.
Best Petrol — six-cylinder refinement, proven reliability.
Most Expensive — the most complex E-Class ever.
Best Diesel — six-cylinder torque, proven reliability.

economicsRule.title: The S214 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£5,500 OM654 recon against a £30,000 S214 sits at 12–18% of the car's value — comfortably safe. Even the M254 at £3,000–£6,000 against a £28,000 E 200 is 11–21% — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold. The S214 is a current-generation car with strong retained values; engine replacement is financially viable across the range. The only exception is the AMG E 53 Hybrid — but if you can afford a £70,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance. For PHEV variants, battery health is the bigger long-term concern.

problems.title: S214 48V Mild-Hybrid System Issues
problems.affectedModels: All S214 mild-hybrid variants (E 200, E 300, E 350, E 450, E 220d, E 450d).
problems.rootCause: The 48V electrical system and integrated starter generator (ISG) are complex components — battery degradation, ISG failure, and software glitches have been reported across Mercedes models with this system. The ISG is located between the transmission and the crankshaft.
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is a new technology for the E-Class — ensure your car has the latest software updates and have the battery health checked at every service. A £300–£600 check is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,000 replacement that follows a complete failure.

problems.title: S214 Plug-In Hybrid Battery Degradation
problems.affectedModels: E 300e, E 350e, E 400e, E 300de, AMG E 53 Hybrid.
problems.rootCause: The 25.4 kWh lithium-ion battery will degrade over time, reducing electric range and potentially requiring replacement. Unlike the MHEV system's small 48V battery, the PHEV battery is large, expensive, and critical to vehicle operation.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a used S214 PHEV, have the battery health checked by a Mercedes dealer — battery degradation is the single biggest long-term cost risk. Mercedes offers an 8-year/100,000-mile battery warranty, but check the terms carefully.

cta.label: Compare S214 PHEV battery health checks →

problems.title: S214 Electrical System & Software Glitches
problems.rootCause: The W214/S214 platform is heavily software-dependent — the MBUX Superscreen, driver assistance systems, and general electrical functions are all controlled by complex software. Glitches and bugs are common on new-generation Mercedes models.
problems.recommendation: Ensure your S214 has the latest software updates from Mercedes — many electrical issues are resolved with a dealer software update. If you're buying used, check the service history for software update records.

problems.title: S214 Self-Levelling Rear Air Suspension (Airmatic) Failure
problems.affectedModels: S214 estates with Airmatic suspension (optional on most models).
problems.rootCause: The S214 estate can be specified with Airmatic air suspension — the rubber air springs perish over time and crack, allowing air to leak. The compressor, ride height sensors, and pipework can also fail. This is a known issue on Mercedes estates with air suspension.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear air suspension inspected on any S214 — a £200–£500 inspection is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000+ replacement that follows a complete system failure. Check for the car sitting low overnight or unevenly, as these are early warning signs.

cta.label: Compare S214 air suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: S214 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — S214 estate shares its MRA2-derived engine bay with the W214 saloon and X214 All-Terrain, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. M256 AMG costs reflect AMG-specific parts, the electric auxiliary compressor, hybrid system components, and specialist labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S214 estate only. W214 Saloon and X214 All-Terrain share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, as fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The S214 estate's 25.4 kWh battery is shared across PHEV variants.
